title: AES, Block Ciphers, and Primitive Boundaries · Language Lens: Crypto Go Aes Api Bounds
summary: A developer calls AES.Encrypt directly on repeated application records, invents padding, and assumes a strong block cipher alone protects a message. Predict the asset, adversary, security property, trust boundary, and falsifying evidence before opening the governing standard. Planned artifact: Add and verify the aes api bounds increment in AES primitive boundary and known-answer harness. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence…
objectives: Validate key and block lengths before using low-level AES APIs and convert panics or errors into bounded caller behavior.
Explain block-cipher keyed permutation behavior and why messages require an approved mode or higher-level construction.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

content.code:
package main

import (
	"crypto/aes"
	"encoding/hex"
	"fmt"
)

func main() {
	key := make([]byte, 16)
	key[0] = 5
	plaintext := []byte("0123456789ABCDEF")
	block, err := aes.NewCipher(key)
	if err != nil { panic(err) }
	ciphertext := make([]byte, aes.BlockSize)
	recovered := make([]byte, aes.BlockSize)
	block.Encrypt(ciphertext, plaintext)
	block.Decrypt(recovered, ciphertext)
	fmt.Printf("block=%d ciphertext=%s recovered=%t message_mode=false\n", aes.BlockSize, hex.EncodeToString(ciphertext), string(recovered) == string(plaintext))
}
